nodes.title: Check if we already have enough arguments
nodes.why: Currying should call the original function only when required arguments are collected.
nodes.alternative: Always return another function and never execute automatically.
nodes.tradeoff: Automatic execution is convenient, but depends on `fn.length` behavior.

nodes.title: Execute original function once threshold is reached
nodes.why: When enough args exist, we can produce the final result right away.
nodes.alternative: Delay execution until an explicit final call signal.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple mental model, but less flexible for advanced custom currying APIs.

nodes.title: Return a function that keeps collecting arguments
nodes.why: This closure stores previous arguments so later calls can continue building the input list.
nodes.alternative: Store partial args in external mutable state.
nodes.tradeoff: Closure approach is clean and local, but creates a new function per partial call.

nodes.title: Use an inner function to hold partial state
nodes.why: The inner function is reused for each step and keeps argument chaining logic in one place.
nodes.alternative: Return anonymous functions directly without naming an inner helper.
nodes.tradeoff: Named helper is easier to read and recurse into, with slightly more code.

nodes.title: Use a short early return for completed input
nodes.why: This keeps the happy path compact: enough args means immediate result.
nodes.alternative: Use a multiline `if` block for consistency.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact style is concise, but multiline can be easier to scan for some readers.

nodes.title: Accumulate with concat for the next step
nodes.why: `concat` merges old and new arguments without mutating previous arrays.
nodes.tradeoff: Both work; `concat` makes array-building intent explicit but is a little less common in modern style.
